era_context: The late 19th century, dominated by European imperial expansion, industrial consolidation, and nationalist movements. The British Empire at its zenith; the German Empire under Bismarck asserting continental dominance; the Ottoman Empire in terminal decline; Russia expanding into Central Asia; the United States industrializing rapidly and beginning Pacific expansion; China fragmenting under foreign pressure and internal instability.

events.title: Second Anglo-Afghan War begins
events.description: The Second Anglo-Afghan War (1878–1880) began in November 1878 when British forces invaded Afghanistan to prevent Russian expansion into the region and to establish British influence over the Afghan government. The war was triggered by the Afghan Amir Sher Ali Khan's refusal to accept a British diplomatic mission. British forces under General Frederick Roberts advanced into Afghanistan, capturing Kandahar and Kabul. The war demonstrated British military superiority but also the challenges of occupying a mountainous, hostile territory. The conflict would reshape Afghan politics and establish the pattern of great power competition over Central Asia.

sources.name: British Indian Army records and military dispatches
British Foreign Office and India Office records
Contemporary British press and military journals

events.certainty_note: The outbreak of the Second Anglo-Afghan War in November 1878 is well-documented in British military and diplomatic records. The date and initial operations are confirmed.

events.title: Congress of Berlin
events.description: The Congress of Berlin (June–July 1878) was convened to settle the territorial disputes arising from the Russo-Turkish War (1877–1878). The congress, presided over by Otto von Bismarck, redrew the map of southeastern Europe and the Ottoman Empire. Key outcomes included the independence of Serbia, Romania, and Montenegro; the cession of Bessarabia to Russia; the cession of Bosnia and Herzegovina to Austria-Hungary; and the confirmation of Cyprus to Britain. The congress represented the apex of Bismarck's diplomatic power and established the framework for European great power relations that would persist until World War I.

sources.name: Congress of Berlin official protocols and treaties
Diplomatic dispatches and correspondence from participating powers
Contemporary European press and political journals

events.certainty_note: The Congress of Berlin in June–July 1878 is extensively documented in diplomatic records and contemporary sources. The dates and major outcomes are confirmed.

events.title: Russo-Turkish War concludes
events.description: The Russo-Turkish War (1877–1878) concluded with the Treaty of San Stefano (3 March 1878) and was subsequently modified by the Congress of Berlin (June–July 1878). The war resulted in a decisive Russian victory and the liberation of several Balkan territories from Ottoman rule. The conflict demonstrated the Ottoman Empire's military decline and accelerated the process of Balkan independence. The war also intensified great power competition in southeastern Europe and contributed to the tensions that would eventually lead to World War I.

sources.name: Russian military records and dispatches
Ottoman government records and diplomatic correspondence
Contemporary European press and military journals

events.certainty_note: The conclusion of the Russo-Turkish War in 1878 is well-documented. The Treaty of San Stefano (3 March 1878) and the Congress of Berlin (June–July 1878) are confirmed historical facts.

events.title: British occupation of Cyprus
events.description: Following the Congress of Berlin, Britain formally occupied Cyprus in July 1878 under the Cyprus Convention with the Ottoman Empire. Though nominally remaining Ottoman territory, Cyprus became a British colony in practice. The occupation was justified as a strategic move to counter Russian expansion in the eastern Mediterranean and to protect British interests in the region. Cyprus would remain under British control until 1960, becoming a key British military base in the Mediterranean.

sources.name: Cyprus Convention (4 June 1878)
British Foreign Office and Colonial Office records
Contemporary British press and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: The British occupation of Cyprus in July 1878 is well-documented in diplomatic records and contemporary sources. The date and terms of the Cyprus Convention are confirmed.

events.title: Flogging of Irish nationalist Michael Davitt
events.description: Michael Davitt, founder of the Irish Land League, was imprisoned in Kilmainham Gaol in 1878 under the Coercion Act. His imprisonment symbolized British repression of Irish nationalism and land reform movements. The Land League's agitation for tenant rights and Irish self-governance intensified during this period, shaping the trajectory of Irish independence movements.
key_figures: Charles Stewart Parnell (Irish Parliamentary Party leader)

sources.name: Kilmainham Gaol records and British Home Office files
Michael Davitt's memoirs and correspondence
Contemporary Irish and British press

events.certainty_note: Davitt's imprisonment in 1878 is well-documented in contemporary sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The Congress of Berlin is often presented as a triumph of Bismarck's diplomatic genius and a stable settlement of European affairs. However, modern scholarship emphasizes that the congress actually created as many problems as it solved. The settlement left numerous ethnic and territorial disputes unresolved, particularly in the Balkans, and the great power competition it was meant to regulate actually intensified in the following decades. Additionally, the congress's treatment of Ottoman territories and the Balkans reflected European imperial interests rather than the self-determination of the peoples affected. The British occupation of Cyprus, justified as a strategic necessity, is sometimes presented as a benign colonial administration, but it represented the imposition of foreign rule on an island with its own political traditions.
historiographic_note: 1878 is exceptionally well-documented in Western sources, particularly British, German, Russian, and Ottoman records. The Congress of Berlin, the Russo-Turkish War, and the Second Anglo-Afghan War are extensively covered in contemporary newspapers, government records, and diplomatic correspondence. However, Ottoman and Balkan perspectives are often mediated through European sources, and the experiences of ordinary people affected by these conflicts are largely absent from the historical record. Russian sources on the Russo-Turkish War are rich but shaped by official imperial narratives. Afghan sources on the Second Anglo-Afghan War are sparse in English-language historiography, and indigenous perspectives are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: The Second Anglo-Afghan War began in 1878 and continued through 1879 with major military campaigns
The Congress of Berlin modified the Treaty of San Stefano and settled the Russo-Turkish War
The Russo-Turkish War's conclusion led to the Congress of Berlin
The British occupation of Cyprus in 1878 lasted until Cypriot independence in 1960
